title: The People Are No Longer Prepared to Believe!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. The page images restore the OCR-omitted opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,` and confirm the damaged figures as `5,723 மெகாவாட்` and `708 மெகாவாட்`. Clearly mechanical OCR character errors and word breaks are repaired, while newspaper reports and political responses remain attributed. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Campaigning in Coimbatore for the local-body elections on 15 September 2014, Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa spoke at length about electricity.
She claimed:

“In the past three years alone, an additional installed capacity of 2,793 MW has been added. Apart from this, 500 MW has been purchased on a medium-term basis. From the first unit of the 1,000 MW Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant, 562 MW is currently being received. Through several new power projects, Tamil Nadu will receive another 737 MW within the next few months.

“The second unit of the 1,000 MW Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant will begin generation by the end of this year, providing 463 MW. During 2014-2015, both 250 MW units of Stage II of the Neyveli Lignite Expansion Project will begin generation, providing Tamil Nadu with 230 MW.

“Long-term purchase agreements have also been entered into to obtain 3,330 MW for fifteen years. Of this, 224 MW is now being received, and the amount will rise to 1,000 MW by December. Altogether, Tamil Nadu will receive an additional 2,430 MW within the 2014-2015 financial year. Including all this, my Government has paved the way to obtain an additional 5,723 MW.”

Most importantly, she said that Tamil Nadu would receive an additional 2,430 MW within the 2014-2015 financial year. There is a saying that even a clever man’s lie lasts only eight days. The Chief Minister’s lie did not last even five!
On 20 September 2014, *Dinamalar* published a major report under the headline, “Is 2,430 MW Possible for Tamil Nadu? Electricity Board Bringing Disrepute to Chief Minister.” It said:
“With only six months remaining in the current financial year, doubts have arisen over whether Tamil Nadu will receive an additional 2,430 MW as Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced.

“At Thoothukudi, NLC and the Electricity Board are jointly constructing a thermal power station with two units of 500 MW each. Tamil Nadu has been allocated 387 MW from it. Generation was planned to begin in March 2012 in the first unit and August 2012 in the second. But generation has not yet begun.”
Yet Jayalalithaa said in Coimbatore that 387 MW would be obtained from there.
The report continued:
“At Vallur, only two units are generating. As commercial generation has not yet begun in the third unit, Tamil Nadu has not received 350 MW.”
Yet Jayalalithaa expects 350 MW from Vallur’s third unit.

“Two nuclear reactors, each with a capacity of 1,000 MW, have been established at Kudankulam. Tamil Nadu has been allocated 1,025 MW. Trial generation is now taking place in the first reactor. The second has not yet begun operating.”
Yet Jayalalithaa said in Coimbatore that Kudankulam would provide an additional 463 MW.
“At Neyveli, two new units of 250 MW each have been established under an expansion project. Because commercial generation has not begun, Tamil Nadu’s receipt of 230 MW has been delayed.”
Yet Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said in Coimbatore that an additional 230 MW was about to arrive from Neyveli.

The report further noted that the Chief Minister had said 224 MW was currently being obtained from private companies and that this would rise to 1,000 MW in December. Though 1,000 MW was due from private companies as early as last February, only 224 MW was being received. Only six months remained in the financial year, while even trial generation had not begun in the second Kudankulam reactor or plants including the NTPL station at Thoothukudi. The newspaper therefore asked whether the promised 2,430 MW could really be obtained within the financial year.
The same newspaper quoted an Energy Department official:

“Electricity Board officials conceal the true power situation from the Chief Minister because they want to earn her approval. Believing them, the Chief Minister tells the public there will be no power cuts. When shortages then force power cuts, the Government is brought into disrepute among the people.”
It is not I who say the Chief Minister’s speech was contrary to fact. I have merely reproduced what *Dinamalar* wrote.

How many times during the past three years has Jayalalithaa given similar assurances to the people of Tamil Nadu? Even before coming to power, the AIADMK said, “Within three months of assuming office, we shall eliminate power cuts.”

After coming to power, replying to the debate on the 2011 Governor’s Address, Jayalalithaa said: “We shall reduce the existing three-hour power cut to two hours. From July, Tamil Nadu’s power shortage will be resolved completely.”

In February 2012, the Minister for the department told *India Today*: “Tamil Nadu will experience a power shortage only from March to May 2012. If these three months are endured, there will be no power shortage in Tamil Nadu.”
Speaking at an event in October 2012, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said the shortage would be completely resolved by the end of 2013.

In a statement read under Assembly Rule 110 on 25 April 2013, she said: “Through the measures we are undertaking, Tamil Nadu will soon become a power-surplus State, and uninterrupted, stable electricity supply to consumers will be ensured.”

Speaking in the Assembly on 25 October 2013, she declared: “No Member need worry. Very soon, extremely soon, Tamil Nadu will be made a power-surplus State. I state this categorically. We shall certainly achieve it.”

Campaigning in the Yercaud by-election on 28 November 2013, Jayalalithaa said: “The Union Government is responsible for the power cuts. I shall successfully overcome even that circumstance, correct Tamil Nadu’s power shortage, and soon create a bright and prosperous condition entirely free of power cuts.”
Again, at the end of May this year, she said: “From 1 June, power cuts will disappear completely.”

Then, on 18 June 2014, *Junior Vikatan* published a ten-page article titled “Tall-Tale Current! Tamil Nadu Suffering in Darkness,” describing the effect of power cuts in every district. To conserve space, I shall give only its subheadings:

“Tirunelveli’s Sorrow”; “Chennai That Frightens Them Away”; “Sudden Power Cuts in Sivaganga”; “Six-Hour Power Cut in Villupuram”; “Vellore Suffering in the Heat”; “Three Phases Shut Down in Cuddalore”; “Tense Tiruvannamalai”; “Ramanathapuram’s Ice Problem”; “Power Shortage Even in the Chief Minister’s Constituency”; “Salem Factories Affected”; “No One Knows the Time in Namakkal”; “Ah, Virudhunagar!”; “Weaving Industry Laid Low in Madurai”; “Power Cut Even at Theni Hospital”; “No Reason Known in Krishnagiri”; and “At Last, Dharmapuri!”
Under these headings, the magazine described the power cuts in every district.
When Jayalalithaa claimed that 2,500 MW had begun to be obtained under her Government, PMK founder Dr Ramadoss replied:

“This 2,500 MW has begun to arrive from projects started during the DMK administration, not from new projects announced by Jayalalithaa. She has also said that agreements have been entered into for long-term purchase of 3,300 MW. From whom is this power to be purchased? Was a proper tender invited? If the Government issues a White Paper, everyone will be able to understand the details.”
That demand for a White Paper dissolved into the air under AIADMK rule!

Jayalalithaa also said: “Recognising that Coimbatore is a city of industrial growth, I ensured that small and micro industries faced no power cuts.” How she ensured this was explained by *Dinamani*, a newspaper that falls over itself every day to support the ruling party. On 20 November 2013, it published a detailed report titled “Eight-Hour Power Cut in Coimbatore: Micro Industries Affected.”

While Tamil Nadu’s power shortage remains in this condition, an important conference of State Power Ministers was held in Delhi on the ninth of this month. Did Tamil Nadu’s Power Minister attend? He did not.

A report published today says Tamil Nadu’s allocation of electricity from Central generating stations has fallen, but Electricity Board officials have made no effort to find a permanent solution. Had the Tamil Nadu Minister attended the Delhi meeting on the ninth and raised the issue, such problems could have been discussed and resolved. But the Minister did not go.

If another party had been in power and one of its Ministers had failed to attend a Union Government meeting during such a crisis, in how many ways would Chief Minister Jayalalithaa have abused and ridiculed them, issuing one of her reckless statements?

Jayalalithaa announced that Tamil Nadu must lead in solar-power generation and would produce 3,000 MW from sunlight within three years. The Government told the Assembly it would produce 1,000 MW in 2013, 1,000 MW in 2014 and 1,000 MW in 2015. Two years have passed since the announcement. What is the position now?

In 2012, the Electricity Board decided to purchase 708 MW from fifty-two investors, but its officials applied to the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission for approval only in March 2013. The Commission issued a comprehensive tariff order for solar power only last Friday, permitting the Board to purchase it at Rs. 7.01 per unit. The national rate is reportedly above Rs. 9.30. Investors therefore consider the prescribed price too low and have reportedly decided to approach the Appellate Tribunal.

Moreover, had the Electricity Board not already sought permission to purchase 708 MW from fifty-two investors? The Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission refused that approval, rejected the petition, and reportedly issued its order on 15 September 2014. That is the sorrowful history of Jayalalithaa’s solar-power project.
If this is the fate of solar generation, do you know the fate of wind power?

Because the southwest monsoon winds began in Tamil Nadu in May, daily wind-power generation stands at 2,500 MW. Windmills can generate up to 3,400 MW a day. Yet artificial restrictions imposed by the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board leave approximately twenty per cent of windmills idle. Electricity is now purchased at Rs. 3.39 per unit.

Rules followed by the Government for several years have placed operators of one or two windmills under severe pressure. There are 1,400 such producers. The Government must invite their representatives and speak with them. Their plants are now idle. Wind-power companies sell electricity to the Government and wait for months to receive payment.
At a time when the Tamil Nadu Government must undertake several action plans, it does nothing. After September, when the windmills stop producing, industrialists are left wondering what they will do.

The Chief Minister concealed all this, read in Coimbatore the deceptive statistics someone had written for her, and returned. As an Electricity Board official stated, certain officials had supplied the Chief Minister with false information. The people of Tamil Nadu have waited long enough, believing what Chief Minister Jayalalithaa says. They are no longer prepared to believe her!
With affection,
M.K.
22 September 2014
